findings.justification: The wp_ajax_mtnc_dismiss_dialog handler verifies a nonce but performs no current_user_can() capability check; any logged-in user who possesses a valid 'mtnc_dismiss_nonce' nonce can invoke update_option('maintenance_meta', ...) and mutate plugin state.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Access Control: AJAX handler without capability check
detection_pattern.description: Matches wp_ajax_ handler registrations; files that have such a handler but no current_user_can() in the corresponding function are suspicious — nonce verification alone is CSRF protection, not authorization.

findings.reachability: Any authenticated user with a valid admin session nonce (admin_init fires for all admin requests)
findings.justification: mtnc_register_settings() calls check_admin_referer() for CSRF but has no current_user_can('manage_options') gate before calling update_option('maintenance_options', ...); an authenticated user who can reach admin-ajax.php or admin.php with a valid nonce can overwrite all plugin settings.

detection_pattern.section_name: Broken Access Control: settings handler with nonce but no capability check
detection_pattern.description: Matches admin_init/form handlers that use check_admin_referer for CSRF but omit a capability check before performing privileged operations such as update_option.

findings.reachability: Any authenticated user; practically only affects multisite or sites with custom roles
findings.justification: current_user_can('administrator') checks a role string rather than a capability (e.g. 'manage_options'); on multisite or sites with custom role plugins this check can be bypassed by users who have administrator-level capabilities but not the 'administrator' role label.

detection_pattern.section_name: Improper capability check: role string instead of capability
detection_pattern.description: Flags current_user_can('administrator') which checks a role name rather than a primitive capability; role-based checks are unreliable on multisite and with custom role plugins, whereas capability-based checks are the correct WordPress security model.

findings.justification: custom_css is sanitized with wp_kses_post() which strips HTML tags but does not sanitize CSS; an admin can store arbitrary CSS including url() data URIs, CSS-based content exfiltration vectors, or @import directives that load external resources.

detection_pattern.section_name: Insufficient CSS sanitization: wp_kses_post on CSS field
detection_pattern.description: wp_kses_post is designed for HTML post content, not CSS; using it on CSS fields allows arbitrary CSS to be stored since it only removes HTML tags. CSS fields should use wp_strip_all_tags or a dedicated CSS sanitizer.
